sections.heading: Given the rapid pace of technological advancements and adoption of general-purpose AI models, obligations
sections.title: AI model providers must comply with AI regulations from 2 August 2025
sections.summary: This section establishes that providers of general-purpose AI models must comply with the AI Regulation starting 2 August 2025. It requires codes of practice to be ready by 2 May 2025 for providers to demonstrate compliance. The AI Office must ensure classification rules are updated with technological developments, and Member States must establish and notify penalties rules by this date for effective implementation.
sections.dtia_summary: AI model providers must implement compliance measures by May 2025 to meet regulatory requirements for data transfers involving AI systems. The section mandates updated classification rules and penalty frameworks that impact cross-border data flows for AI systems.

requirements.text: Providers of general-purpose AI models must comply with AI regulations from 2 August 2025
Codes of practice must be ready by 2 May 2025 for providers to demonstrate compliance
AI Office must ensure classification rules and procedures are updated with technological developments
Member States must lay down and notify to the Commission rules on penalties including administrative fines
Member States must ensure penalties rules are properly and effectively implemented by 2 August 2025

sections.source_quote: Given the rapid pace of technological advancements and adoption of general-purpose AI models, obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models should apply from 2 August 2025. Codes of practice should be ready by 2 May 2025 in view of enabling providers to demonstrate compliance on time.
sections.relevance_reason: AI regulation compliance requires cross-border data transfer and security measures

sections.heading: This Regulation lays down:
sections.title: AI model development description
sections.summary: Requires providers to describe AI model elements and development processes, including technical means for integration, input/output modalities, and data used for training, testing, and validation. This information is critical for assessing model capabilities and risks.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates detailed descriptions of AI model development processes to ensure transparency and compliance with data protection and security requirements.

requirements.text: Provider must describe technical means required for general-purpose AI model integration
Provider must describe input/output modalities and maximum size of data
Provider must describe data used for training, testing, and validation including type, provenance, and curation methodologies

sections.source_quote: 2. A description of the elements of the model and of the process for its development, including: (a) the technical means (e.g. instructions for use, infrastructure, tools) required for the general-purpose AI model to be integrated into AI systems; (b) the modality (e.g. text, image, etc.) and format of the inputs and outputs and their maximum size (e.g. context window length, etc.); (c) information on the data used for training, testing and validation, where applicable, including the type and provenance of data and curation methodologies.

sections.title: Annual reporting of administrative fines by European Data Protection Supervisor
sections.summary: The European Data Protection Supervisor must annually report to the Commission all administrative fines imposed and any litigation or judicial proceedings initiated under this Regulation. This reporting obligation applies to the Supervisor and covers fines related to data protection violations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates annual reporting of fines, which affects DTIA by requiring assessment of fine imposition mechanisms and their impact on data transfer compliance.

requirements.text: European Data Protection Supervisor shall notify Commission annually of administrative fines imposed
European Data Protection Supervisor shall notify Commission annually of litigation or judicial proceedings initiated

sections.source_quote: The European Data Protection Supervisor shall, on an annual basis, notify the Commission of the administrative fines it has imposed pursuant to this Article and of any litigation or judicial proceedings it has initiated.

sections.title: Amendment considerations for sectoral harmonisation legislation
sections.summary: Amendments to the Regulation that affect sectoral Union harmonisation legislation must consider sector-specific regulatory nuances, existing governance structures, conformity assessment procedures, and enforcement mechanisms established within each sector. These amendments must account for the unique regulatory frameworks and authorities operating within each sector.

requirements.text: Amendments to this Regulation that affect sectoral Union harmonisation legislation must consider regulatory specificities of each sector

sections.source_quote: Any amendment to this Regulation pursuant to paragraph 10, or relevant delegated or implementing acts, which concerns sectoral Union harmonisation legislation listed in Section B of Annex I shall take into account the regulatory specificities of each sector, and the existing governance, conformity assessment and enforcement mechanisms and authorities established therein.

Fines consider the nature, gravity, and duration of infringements and may be adjusted based on commitments made.

sections.title: Technical documentation control for AI systems
sections.summary: Providers must submit technical documentation to notified bodies for assessment of AI systems intended for market placement. Notified bodies may require additional evidence or tests, and have access to training data sets with security safeguards. For high-risk AI systems, notified bodies can access training models with IP and trade secret protections. Notified bodies issue certificates of conformity or refusal with detailed reasons.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires detailed technical documentation for AI systems, including access to training data and models with safeguards. This impacts DTIA by mandating specific documentation and assessment processes for high-risk AI systems.

Notified body must issue Union technical documentation assessment certificate if AI system conforms

Notified body must provide detailed reasons for refusal of certificate
Provider must retrain AI system if data used does not meet requirements

This system supplements the European Criminal Records Information System and amends Regulation (EU) 2018/1726.

sections.title: Reporting scope for high-risk AI systems
sections.summary: This section limits the reporting of serious incidents to those specified in Article 3(49)(c) for high-risk AI systems placed on the market or put into service by providers subject to equivalent reporting obligations under Union legislative instruments. The obligation applies to providers of high-risk AI systems and restricts incident reporting to specific incident types.

requirements.text: Reporting of serious incidents shall be limited to those referred to in Article 3, point (49)(c)

sections.source_quote: For high-risk AI systems referred to in Annex III that are placed on the market or put into service by providers that are subject to Union legislative instruments laying down reporting obligations equivalent to those set out in this Regulation, the notification of serious incidents shall be limited to those referred to in Article 3, point (49)(c).

sections.title: Notification of serious incidents for high-risk AI systems
sections.summary: For high-risk AI systems that are safety components of devices or themselves devices covered by EU regulations 2017/745 and 2017/746, serious incident notifications must be limited to those specified in Article 3(49)(c) and submitted to the national competent authority chosen by Member States where the incident occurred.

sections.title: Fines for general-purpose AI model providers
sections.summary: The Commission may impose fines up to 3% of annual worldwide turnover or EUR 15 million for providers of general-purpose AI models who intentionally or negligently violate data protection rules, fail to provide requested documents, or do not comply with safety measures. Fines consider the nature, gravity, and duration of infringements and may be adjusted based on commitments made.

sections.title: Technical documentation control for AI systems
sections.summary: Providers must submit technical documentation to notified bodies for assessment of AI systems intended for market placement. Notified bodies may require additional evidence or tests, and have access to training data sets with security safeguards. For high-risk AI systems, notified bodies can access training models with IP and trade secret protections. Notified bodies issue certificates of conformity or refusal with detailed reasons.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires detailed technical documentation for AI systems, including access to training data and models with safeguards. This impacts DTIA by mandating specific documentation and assessment processes for high-risk AI systems.

sections.title: Justice and democratic processes AI systems
sections.summary: This section defines AI systems used in judicial processes, alternative dispute resolution, and election-related activities. These systems must not influence election outcomes or voting behavior without direct exposure to individuals, and must be designed to ensure transparency and fairness in legal proceedings.

sections.source_quote: Administration of justice and democratic processes: (a) AI systems intended to be used by a judicial authority or on their behalf to assist a judicial authority in researching and interpreting facts and the law and in applying the law to a concrete set of facts, or to be used in a similar way in alternative dispute resolution; (b) AI systems intended to be used for influencing the outcome of an election or referendum or the voting behaviour of natural persons in the exercise of their vote in elections or referenda. This does not include AI systems to the output of which natural persons are not directly exposed, such as tools used to organise, optimise or structure political campaigns from an administrative or logistical point of view.

sections.source_quote: National competent authorities shall submit annual reports to the AI Office and to the Board, from one year after the establishment of the AI regulatory sandbox and every year thereafter until its termination, and a final report. sections.source_quote: Administration of justice and democratic processes: (a) AI systems intended to be used by a judicial authority or on their behalf to assist a judicial authority in researching and interpreting facts and the law and in applying the law to a concrete set of facts, or to be used in a similar way in alternative dispute resolution; (b) AI systems intended to be used for influencing the outcome of an election or referendum or the voting behaviour of natural persons in the exercise of their vote in elections or referenda. sections.summary: This section defines AI systems used in judicial processes, alternative dispute resolution, and election-related activities. sections.source_quote: Administration of justice and democratic processes: (a) AI systems intended to be used by a judicial authority or on their behalf to assist a judicial authority in researching and interpreting facts and the law and in applying the law to a concrete set of facts, or to be used in a similar way in alternative dispute resolution; (b) AI systems intended to be used for influencing the outcome of an election or referendum or the voting behaviour of natural persons in the exercise of their vote in elections or referenda. This does not include AI systems to the output of which natural persons are not directly exposed, such as tools used to organise, optimise or structure political campaigns from an administrative or logistical point of view.
